access.accessMethod: University of Michigan item landing page and page-image/text interface
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME item AHA2735.0001.001
access.format: Web text and linked page images
notes: The landing page was reachable in an interactive browser, but automated direct download was blocked by provider anti-bot controls; the exact XML artifact is preserved through the pinned CME repository.

rights.basis: The current U-M item interface labels the item Public Domain and applies RightsStatements.org NoC-US. The XML header itself retains a generic where-applicable copyright caution, so this record preserves the current item assertion without projecting it onto every scan-layer contract term.
rights.component: U-M OCR text, structural encoding, and linked digital-item presentation

entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 encoding of Furnivall's title page, English Handlyng Synne, parallel French portions, editorial notes, pagination, and omission placeholders, with 414 linked 600-dpi page images in the U-M service.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2735.0001.001
notes: The XML explicitly says no corrections or content editing were performed beyond basic structural encoding.
Five front images and ten back images are represented as omitted matter; the title page is that of Part I.

rights.basis: The current U-M item interface labels the item Public Domain and applies RightsStatements.org NoC-US. The XML header itself retains a generic where-applicable copyright caution, so this record preserves the current item assertion without projecting it onto every scan-layer contract term.
rights.component: U-M OCR text, structural encoding, and linked digital-item presentation

access.accessMethod: Internet Archive bound and separate-part scans modeled as distinct facsimile entities
access.entityLabel: Furnivall's re-edited Handlyng Synne, EETS OS 119 and 123 (1901–1903)

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Robert of Brunne's ‘Handlyng Synne,’ A.D. 1303, with Those Parts of the Anglo-French Treatise on Which It Was Founded, William of Wadington's ‘Manuel des Pechiez,’ Parts I–II, Early English Text Society, Original Series 119 and 123 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1901–1903).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 119 and 123
bibliographic.title: Robert of Brunne's ‘Handlyng Synne,’ A.D. 1303, with Those Parts of the Anglo-French Treatise on Which It Was Founded, William of Wadington's ‘Manuel des Pechiez’

entities.description: The composite two-part scholarly source of the U-M encoding, printing Mannyng's English poem with corresponding portions of William of Waddington's Anglo-French Manuel des péchés and an extensive variant apparatus.
entities.label: Furnivall's re-edited Handlyng Synne, EETS OS 119 and 123 (1901–1903)
notes: Contemporary title matter projected a Part III containing introduction, notes, glossary, and index; current bibliographic evidence supports only the two textual volumes OS 119 and 123 represented here.

rights.basis: Both volumes were published before 1930 and are in the U.S. public domain under the publication-date rules summarized by the U.S. Copyright Office; the medieval works are separately public-domain textual layers.
rights.component: 1901–1903 edition text, apparatus, and typographic arrangement

access.accessMethod: British Library manuscript-item descriptions and Furnivall's cached parallel-text edition
access.entityLabel: William of Waddington's Manuel des péchés

entities.description: The abstract thirteenth-century Anglo-French penitential work translated and extensively adapted by Mannyng. This conceptual source-work entity is distinct from Furnivall's edited French text and from every surviving manuscript.
entities.label: William of Waddington's Manuel des péchés
notes: The traditional attribution to Robert Grosseteste in Mannyng's English opening and older cataloging is not adopted; modern identification assigns Manuel des péchés to William of Waddington and distinguishes Grosseteste's Le Chasteau d'Amour.

rights.basis: The medieval verbal work predates modern copyright; later editions, manuscript images, and catalog records are separate layers.
rights.component: Thirteenth-century Anglo-French verbal work

access.accessMethod: British Library reading-room access subject to current procedures; official catalog reports no digital copy
access.entityLabel: London, British Library, Harley MS 1701

entities.description: The principal English running-text witness for Furnivall's 1901–1903 edition; Handlyng Synne occupies ff. 1r–84r.

holding.notes: Handlyng Synne ff. 1r–84r; current BL record reports no digital copy available.

entities.label: London, British Library, Harley MS 1701

rights.basis: This entity is the material manuscript witness, not an abstract copyrightable work. Custody, reading-room access, and provider conditions are distinct from copyright. Public-domain conclusions here are limited to the medieval works and passages actually published in the public-domain editions; unprinted variants are not separately assessed.
rights.component: Physical manuscript witness Harley MS 1701

holding.notes: Handlyng Synne begins f. 1; item 2 begins f. 80.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Bodl. 415

access.accessMethod: Book a research appointment with the Dulwich College Archive by emailing archives@dulwich.org.uk; appointments are limited to Wednesdays 10:00–16:00 subject to availability
access.entityLabel: Dulwich College, MS 24

localCopies.label: Dulwich College Archive research access page

notes: No public page-image facsimile of Dulwich MS 24 was found; the current official route is an archive enquiry and appointment. In-depth research may incur a fee.

entities.description: An incomplete early-fifteenth-century paper fragment collated by Furnivall. Furnivall's direct note reports extant text through English line 2894 followed by the catchwords ‘ȝoure wikkid vowys’; the 1881 catalogue reports lines 1–2897.

holding.notes: Described in the 1881 Dulwich manuscript catalogue; direct modern manuscript access was not established.

entities.label: Dulwich College, MS 24

notes: The historical catalogue reports the surname Rose at f. 16.

rights.basis: This entity is the material manuscript fragment, not an abstract copyrightable work. Custody, appointment access, and provider conditions are distinct from copyright. Public-domain conclusions here are limited to the medieval work and passages actually published in the public-domain editions; unprinted variants are not separately assessed.
rights.component: Physical manuscript fragment Dulwich MS 24

next_steps: Obtain digitization provenance or image-level metadata from the University of Michigan.
Do not identify the U-M carrier with any Internet Archive scan without copy-specific evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which exact physical two-volume or bound copy supplied U-M's 414 page images and OCR?

next_steps: Review source-critical scholarship and collate distinctive order and readings across the wider French tradition.
Continue to distinguish Furnivall's Harley 273 editorial base from Mannyng's unidentified medieval exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Can Mannyng's exact Manuel des péchés exemplar or exemplar family be identified?

next_steps: Request direct manuscript inspection through the current Dulwich College Archive appointment route.
Retain Furnivall's direct 2894-plus-catchwords statement and Blanch's historical 1–2897 report as distinct evidence until resolved.
openQuestions.question: Does Dulwich MS 24 end at line 2894 plus catchwords or at line 2897?

next_steps: Check EETS archival and bibliographic records for unpublished introduction, notes, glossary, or index material.
Do not treat the projected Part III as a missing continuation of the complete English text in Parts I–II.
openQuestions.question: Was any material for Furnivall's advertised Part III issued elsewhere or left in archival form?

reviewNotes: Handlyng Synne, Manuel des péchés, manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, facsimiles, digital encoding, and repository artifact are separate entities and layers.
Mannyng is author, translator, and adapter of the English work; William of Waddington is author of the French source work; Furnivall is editor, transcriber, and collator of the modern edition.
The English text's medieval attribution of the French source to Robert Grosseteste is historically preserved but not adopted as current authorship metadata.

Harley 1701 and Harley 273 are principal running-text witnesses for Furnivall's English and French texts respectively. Collation and selected supply relations do not turn the other manuscripts into coequal bases.
The 414-image U-M carrier is not identified with the 1901 Part I, 1903 Part II, or separately cached bound Internet Archive scans.
Complete IIIF means every canvas exposed by the cited provider manifest at the selected profile, not a preservation master and not permission to redistribute.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned copy of University of Michigan item AHA2735.0001.001: a 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived Level 1 encoding of Frederick J. Furnivall's EETS OS 119 and 123 parallel edition (1901–1903). The cataloged medieval work is Robert Mannyng's Handlyng Synne, begun in 1303 as a substantial Middle English translation and adaptation of William of Waddington's Anglo-French Manuel des péchés. Furnivall's English running text principally transcribes BL Harley MS 1701, collating Bodley 415 and the Dulwich 24 fragment and occasionally supplying Harley omissions; his French parallel text principally transcribes BL Harley MS 273, with Harley 4657 variants and supplies and selected comparison against Arundel 288 and Royal 20 B XIV. The Vernon Manuscript preserves a collateral extract of English lines 9891–10808. Separate and bound Internet Archive carriers, complete provider IIIF bundles for Harley 4657 and Vernon, all direct catalog sources, and publication-rights evidence are cached with fixity, presence, and work-portion declarations. The exact U-M source copy remains unidentified and is not conflated with any cached Internet Archive scan.

catalogSummary.author: Robert Mannyng
catalogSummary.region: England; associated with Sempringham Priory and Bourne in Kesteven, Lincolnshire
catalogSummary.title: Handlyng Synne

catalogingSubject.label: Handlyng Synne
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is Robert Mannyng's Middle English penitential poem Handlyng Synne, an expansive translation and adaptation of William of Waddington's Anglo-French Manuel des péchés, internally dated 1303. It is not William's source work, a particular manuscript witness, Frederick J. Furnivall's 1862 or 1901–1903 parallel-text editions, the University of Michigan OCR encoding, its bound page-image carrier, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Handlyng Synne
contentParts.summary: Container for the six major doctrinal and penitential sections of Mannyng's poem.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Invocation, plan, audience, autobiographical setting, date, and explanation of the translated title.

contentParts.label: The Ten Commandments
contentParts.summary: Ten commandment sections combining doctrinal explanation, moral interrogation, and exempla.

contentParts.label: The Seven Deadly Sins
contentParts.summary: Pride, anger, envy, sloth, covetousness, gluttony, and lechery, with moral teaching and exempla.

contentParts.label: Sacrilege
contentParts.summary: Instruction on offenses against churches, clergy, holy things, and ecclesiastical rights.

contentParts.label: The Seven Sacraments
contentParts.summary: Baptism, confirmation, the Eucharist, penance, holy orders, marriage, and extreme unction.

contentParts.label: Shrift and Confession

locators.value: pp. 350–396; lines 11304–12628 plus two unnumbered closing lines

contentParts.summary: A prologue followed by twelve points of shrift, eight graces bestowed by shrift, and properties of confession.

notes: Mannyng states that he began the English rhyme in the year of grace 1303; this dates the work's undertaking, not any surviving manuscript.

scope.description: Furnivall's re-edited parallel-text publication, not a revision by Mannyng

extent.description: Six major intellectual sections in five BODY DIV1 containers. The XML contains 1,208 LG and 12,649 L elements across printed pp. 1–396. Furnivall's final printed English line number is 12,628, followed by two unnumbered closing lines; the encoded L count and printed terminal numbering measure different layers and are not normalized into one asserted intrinsic line total. The source carrier has 414 PB elements, including omitted front and back matter.

formStatements.description: A long narrative-didactic poem represented in 12,649 BODY L elements grouped into 1,208 LG containers.
Predominantly short rhyming couplets; XML LG boundaries are encoding groups and are not treated as an intrinsic stanza count.

scope.description: Exempla are distributed throughout the doctrinal sequence rather than forming a separate anthology
Commandments, biblical and liturgical phrases, rubrics, and quotations embedded throughout the English text

notes: The Anglo-French parallel text in Furnivall's edition belongs to Manuel des péchés and is not counted as a secondary language or textual extent of Handlyng Synne.

The medieval English opening attributes the French source to Robert Grosseteste. Modern cataloging identifies William of Waddington as author of Manuel des péchés; Grosseteste is therefore retained only as a historical false attribution in the textual record.

Furnivall's title matter advertised a projected Part III of introduction, notes, glossary, and index. The represented textual publication is complete in EETS OS 119 and 123; no projected Part III is folded into this work record.

next_steps: Obtain U-M digitization provenance or scan metadata before identifying the encoding's carrier with any Internet Archive copy.
openQuestions.question: Which exact physical copy supplied the University of Michigan's 414 page images and OCR?

next_steps: Consult source-critical scholarship across the Manuel des péchés tradition without equating Furnivall's Harley 273 base text with Mannyng's lost exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Can Mannyng's exact Anglo-French exemplar or exemplar family be identified more securely?

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript directly: Furnivall reports extant text through line 2894 plus catchwords, while the historical catalogue reports lines 1–2897.
openQuestions.question: How should the Dulwich 24 fragment's endpoint be reconciled between Furnivall's direct statement and the 1881 catalogue?

notes: The autobiographical passage strongly associates composition with the priory but does not explicitly name the place at which every line was composed.

scope.description: Mannyng addresses the fellowship of Sempringham and says that he had lived at the priory for fifteen years when he began the poem
Mannyng identifies himself with Brunne or Bourne in Kesteven, six miles from Sempringham
Authorship of the Middle English poem, including its English organization, additions, and exempla
Translation from an Anglo-French form of William of Waddington's Manuel des péchés
Substantial adaptation, reorganization, expansion, and localization rather than a line-for-line translation
The 1862 first edition and the re-edited 1901–1903 parallel-text edition represented by the U-M encoding
English running text from Harley 1701 with selected supplies and French parallel text principally from Harley 273
English variants from Bodley 415 and Dulwich 24 and French variants and selected supplies from Harley 4657, Arundel 288, and Royal 20 B XIV

subjects.label: Sacrilege and reverence for the Church

scope.description: Narrative examples illustrating sins, repentance, punishment, sacramental teaching, and salvation

summaries.value: Robert Mannyng's Handlyng Synne is a long Middle English penitential poem begun in 1303 for a lay audience. Translating and extensively adapting William of Waddington's Anglo-French Manuel des péchés, it organizes moral and pastoral instruction around the Ten Commandments, seven deadly sins, sacrilege, seven sacraments, and confession, repeatedly using memorable exempla to expose sin and promote amendment.
Prologue; the Ten Commandments; the seven deadly sins; sacrilege; the seven sacraments; and a final confession sequence comprising a prologue, twelve points, eight graces, and properties of shrift.

The U-M XML encodes Furnivall's English text and parallel French source portions within a 1901–1903 edition carrier, but this record describes Mannyng's English work. The parallel Manuel des péchés is modeled as a related source work and in lineage, not merged into the target's language or extent.

titles.supplied_by: Robert Mannyng's authorial explanation of the translated title and established modern usage

work.summary: Robert Mannyng's Handlyng Synne is a long Middle English penitential poem begun in 1303 for a lay audience. Translating and extensively adapting William of Waddington's Anglo-French Manuel des péchés, it organizes moral and pastoral instruction around the Ten Commandments, seven deadly sins, sacrilege, seven sacraments, and confession, repeatedly using memorable exempla to expose sin and promote amendment.
